name: GHC Cmm
summary: GHC's lowest IR before native codegen — a portable assembly language: typed (bits32, bits64, float64), with explicit calling conventions, machine word operations, and labeled blocks. Flat but target-independent; the same Cmm lowers to x86-64, ARM64, or via the LLVM backend.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
GHC's Cmm ("C--", or "C minus minus") is the lowest IR before native code generation. It is a portable assembly language: typed (`bits32`, `bits64`, `float64`), with explicit calling conventions, machine-word operations, and labelled blocks. Cmm is flat (no high-level constructs) but still target-independent — the same Cmm can lower to x86-64, ARM64, or via the LLVM backend.

The IR is the Cmm-CMM-level optimisation substrate: block layout, register allocation hints, peephole rewrites, and tail-call shaping all operate on Cmm before native codegen.

sections.title: Role in GHC's multi-level pipeline
sections.content:
Cmm's role in GHC's pipeline is the bottom of the cascade: Core (high-level functional, typed lambda) → STG (Spineless Tagless G-machine, operational semantics for laziness) → Cmm (portable assembly) → native or LLVM. Each level discards information the next doesn't need.

Cmm's specific job is to bridge STG's laziness-aware operational model and the target machine. STG describes what closures and thunks *are*; Cmm describes how to allocate, evaluate, and update them at the instruction level — but without committing to one ISA.

sections.title: Design lessons
sections.content:
Cmm illustrates that a target-independent assembly-like layer is a viable alternative to going directly to LLVM. GHC has used Cmm to maintain native-codegen pipelines independent of the LLVM ecosystem, while also retaining LLVM as an alternative backend that consumes Cmm.

For a language designer, the choice between an in-house Cmm-style flat IR and a direct LLVM lowering is the same one OCaml made (Cmm-style) versus Rust (LLVM-only): an in-house IR keeps fast paths for whole-program features (register pinning, custom calling conventions, GC barriers) that LLVM optimisations would obscure, at the cost of multi-target backend maintenance.
